plainMeaning: This verse names the heavens of the upper world one above another — from Saudharma up to the five supreme Anuttara vimānas — and begins counting the eternal Jina-palaces in each: thirty-two, twenty-eight, twelve, eight and four lakh.
recitation: (ḍhāḷa-chhaṭṭhī) cāla / sohama īśāna sanatakumāra e, / māhinda baṁbha re lāntaka sāra e ॥ / truṭaka / sāra śukra ane sahasāraha, ānata prāṇata āraṇā. / acyuta navagraiveyaka trika tihāṁ pañca anuttara tāraṇā. / anukrame prāsāda kahīe, lākha sahasa sata saṅkhyā. / battīsa aṭṭhāvīsa bāraha, aṭh cau lakkha akkhayā.

wordByWord.notes: Structural heading marking the start of Dhāla 6, the upper-world (ūrdhva-loka) census.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati cāla 'gait, running tune' < Skt. cala 'moving'
wordByWord.notes: A musical-prosodic marker; the dhāla alternates a cāla part and a truṭak part.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sohamma < Skt. saudharma, the first kalpa
wordByWord.notes: Lowest of the kalpa heavens, abode of Śakra (Indra).

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati / Apabhraṁśa enclitic e, deictic-emphatic
wordByWord.notes: Line-end refrain syllable; carries no lexical meaning.
Filler for metre and song; not a name.
Praise-word closing the cāla line; deliberately echoed at the start of the truṭak (the truṭak's chain-rhyme device).
Repeated line-end refrain.
Part of the book's text per its orthography.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati truṭak, a quick chain-rhymed metre
wordByWord.notes: Its convention is to begin by repeating the cāla's last word (here sāra).
Chain-rhyme repetition of the previous line's final word; carries the sense forward to Śukra.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati ane 'and' < Skt. anyat / Apabhraṁśa aṇṇa
wordByWord.notes: Coordinating conjunction.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sahasāra < Skt. sahasrāra; final -ha is a metrical/Apabhraṁśa extension

wordByWord.notes: Last of the twelve kalpas; above it lie the kalpātīta realms.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. nava 'nine' + graiveyaka 'of the neck' (from grīvā), so named as the 'neck' of the cosmic Puruṣa
wordByWord.notes: Kalpātīta realm just above Acyuta.
The nine graiveyakas are grouped as three triads — lower, middle and upper.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. an-uttara 'than which there is none higher'
wordByWord.notes: The topmost kalpātīta heaven, just below the siddha-śilā.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. tāraṇa 'that which ferries across' < √tṝ 'to cross'
wordByWord.notes: Rhyme-word praising the supreme heavens as near to liberation; from the Anuttara a being is at most two births from mokṣa.
Signals that the numbers that follow map onto the heavens just listed, in order.
Here the eternal Jina-caityas crowning the heavenly vimānas; their tally equals the tally of vimānas.
First-person plural / passive of recitation.
Units header: the largest tier of the count.
Middle tier (the thousands figures fall in the next verse).
Smallest tier of the tally.
Declares the whole list as a precise reckoning.
32 lakh vimāna-palaces in Saudharma.
28 lakh in Īśāna.
12 lakh in Sanatkumāra.
8 lakh in Māhendra.
4 lakh in Brahmaloka.
The unit applied to all five preceding figures (32/28/12/8/4 lakh).
Stresses that these are śāśvata (ever-existing) shrines; a variant reading akkhayā < ākhyāta 'declared' is possible, but akṣaya better fits the eternal-temple theme.

literalTranslation: (Dhāla six — cāla:) Saudharma, Īśāna, Sanatkumāra; Māhendra, Brahma, and excellent Lāntaka. (Truṭak:) excellent Śukra and Sahasrāra, Ānata, Prāṇata, Āraṇa; Acyuta, the nine graiveyakas in three triads, and there the five supreme Anuttara, the deliverers. In order their palaces are recounted, by lakhs, thousands and hundreds: thirty-two, twenty-eight, twelve, eight, four — lakhs, imperishable.
idiomaticTranslation: Now the eye climbs the upper heavens, rung upon rung: Saudharma, Īśāna, Sanatkumāra; Māhendra, Brahma, glorious Lāntaka; glorious Śukra and Sahasrāra; Ānata, Prāṇata, Āraṇa, Acyuta; then the nine Graiveyakas in their three triads, and crowning all, the five supreme Anuttara that carry the soul across. Taken in order, their eternal Jina-palaces are told by lakhs, thousands and hundreds — thirty-two, twenty-eight, twelve, eight, and four lakh, undying and ever-standing.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Having finished the lower world, the hymn now lifts the worshipper's gaze straight up the spine of the cosmos. The verse is essentially a vertical roll-call of the heavens, recited as a single ascending breath: the twelve kalpas (Saudharma through Acyuta), then the two kalpātīta tiers — the nine Graiveyakas 'in three triads' (trika) and the five Anuttara at the very top. The form mirrors the content: the truṭak's chain-rhyme, where the cāla's last word sāra is caught up again to begin the next line, makes the list itself feel like one realm linking seamlessly into the next, the way the heavens stack without gap. Two epithets do quiet doctrinal work. The Anuttara are called tāraṇā, 'the deliverers' — fitting, since a soul reaching the Anuttara is at most a birth or two from mokṣa. And the whole tally is sealed as akkhayā, 'imperishable': these are not built temples but śāśvata shrines that have always stood and always will. The verse then opens the census proper. 'In order (anukrame) the palaces are recounted, by lakhs, thousands and hundreds' is a deliberate signpost: the numbers that follow are to be read against the heavens just named, in sequence. The first five figures land here — 32, 28, 12, 8, 4 lakh — for Saudharma, Īśāna, Sanatkumāra, Māhendra and Brahma; the thousands- and hundreds-tier counts for the remaining heavens spill into the next verse, which is exactly why this one ends mid-reckoning on lakkha. The reciter is thus left leaning forward into the following lines.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The 'palaces' (prāsāda) are the eternal Jina-caityas that, in Jaina cosmography, crown the vimānas of every heaven; counting the palaces is counting the vimānas, and the figures the verse gives — 32, 28, 12, 8, 4 lakh — are precisely the canonical vimāna-tallies of the first five kalpas in the Tattvārtha tradition. The point is not arithmetic but darśana-strengthening: the eternal, uncreated Jina-shrine exists in untold millions even in realms no human will ever enter, so the Jina is honoured wholly apart from any human act of building. The śāśvata/aśāśvata distinction (here flagged by akkhayā) and the kalpa vs. kalpātīta division (the twelve named kalpas versus the Graiveyaka–Anuttara) are the load-bearing categories the count rests on.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited at dawn, this verse is a vertical meditation: the practitioner lets the names climb, Saudharma to Anuttara, bowing in spirit to an eternal Jina-temple atop each heaven. The lakh-figures are meant to dissolve the small self in awe, not to be summed — let the numbers widen the heart, then carry that steadied reverence into the day.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 4.3–4.4 (the vaimānika devas: kalpopapanna and kalpātīta; order of the heavens)
Bṛhat-saṅgrahaṇī / Saṅgrahaṇī-ratna (vimāna-counts of the kalpas: 32, 28, 12, 8, 4 lakh …)

sources.title: Vaimānika — Jainism definition (citing Tattvārthasūtra 4.3 and the order of the twelve kalpas)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming the sequence Saudharma, Īśāna, Sanatkumāra, Māhendra, Brahmaloka, Lāntaka, Mahāśukra, Sahasrāra, Ānata, Prāṇata, Āraṇa, Acyuta, and the kalpopapanna/kalpātīta division.

sources.title: Graiveyaka — Jainism definition (kalpātīta subclass)
sources.consultedFor: Identifying the nine graiveyaka as kalpātīta vimānas above Acyuta and the etymology from grīvā ('neck').

sources.title: Anuttara — Jainism definition
sources.consultedFor: The five Anuttara as the supreme vimānas and the sense an-uttara 'than which none is higher'.

sources.title: Prāsāda — definitions (Sanskrit/architecture)
sources.consultedFor: Glossing prāsāda as 'lofty mansion / temple' for the celestial Jina-palaces.

notes: Token spellings (sohama, baṁbha, sahasāraha, āraṇā, battīsa, akkhayā, etc.) are kept verbatim per the book's orthography, including its Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa metrical forms (e.g. final -ha in sahasāraha, -ā in āraṇā for rhyme). The five lakh-figures (32/28/12/8/4) align with the canonical vimāna-counts of the first five kalpas (Saudharma–Brahma); the verse stops mid-census, with the thousands/hundreds tiers (Lāntaka 50,000; Mahāśukra 40,000; Sahasrāra 6,000; Ānata–Āraṇa hundreds; graiveyaka 111; anuttara 5) continuing in the next verse, so 'sahasa/sata' here are headers, not yet applied. akkhayā is read as akṣaya 'imperishable' (suiting the śāśvata-caitya theme); a minority reading ākhyāta 'declared' is possible — flagged as uncertain. tāraṇā is taken as an epithet 'delivering/saving' rather than a place-name; this is interpretive. Vimāna-tallies are traditional cosmographic figures preserved as printed, not independently audited.
